verses.text: When all these things are come upon thee — the blessing and the curse — and thou shalt call them to mind among all the nations whither the LORD hath driven thee, and shalt return unto the LORD with all thy heart and soul, thou and thy children — then the LORD will turn thy captivity, gather thee from all the nations. If any be driven out unto the outmost parts of heaven, from thence will the LORD thy God gather thee, bring thee into the land. Guidance: Hope after the curse. Even the farthest exile is not the end. Memory ('call them to mind') + return ('with all thy heart') triggers restoration. The God who scattered will gather — from the outmost parts of heaven, not one missed. Israel's worst chapter does not get the last word.

The LORD thy God will circumcise thine heart, and the heart of thy seed, to love the LORD with all thine heart, with all thy soul, that thou mayest live. Guidance: The deepest promise in the Pentateuch. The outward circumcision is the sign; the inward one is the cure. God Himself does the surgery — He gives a heart that can finally love Him with everything. Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Paul will all return to this verse. Sometimes salvation is what God does INSIDE you, after every external command has failed.

The LORD will put all these curses upon thine enemies, on them that persecuted thee. Thou shalt return, obey, do all His commandments. The LORD will make thee plenteous in every work of thine hand, the fruit of thy body, of cattle, of land, for good: for the LORD will again rejoice over thee for good, as He rejoiced over thy fathers — if thou shalt hearken with all thy heart and all thy soul. Guidance: The curses don't disappear; they get redirected. God is not soft about justice — but His people, once returned, get the harvest blessings again. And the verb 'rejoice' is doubled: He WILL rejoice over you, as He rejoiced over the fathers. Recovery is full.

This commandment which I command thee this day is not hidden, neither far off. It is not in heaven, that thou shouldest say, Who shall go up to heaven, bring it unto us, that we may hear it? Neither beyond the sea, that thou shouldest say, Who shall go over the sea? But the word is very nigh unto thee, in thy mouth, in thy heart, that thou mayest do it. Guidance: A famous answer to the most common excuse. The word is not in some unreachable library, not on the other side of the ocean — it is already in your mouth and your heart. Paul will read this verse straight through to faith in Christ (Romans 10). The word God speaks does not require a heroic quest; it just asks to be obeyed.

See, I have set before thee this day life and good, death and evil; in that I command thee to love the LORD thy God, walk in His ways, keep His commandments, statutes, judgments, that thou mayest live. But if thine heart turn away, thou wilt not hear, but shalt be drawn away, worship other gods — I denounce unto you this day, that ye shall surely perish, not prolong your days upon the land. Guidance: Two doors, both wide open. Loving the LORD is not a feeling we wait to summon; it is the door to life. Turning away is not a quiet drift; God names it as 'surely perish.' The choice is not pretend-neutral. The God who pleads is the God who tells the truth about what each path holds.

I call heaven and earth to record this day, that I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing: therefore choose life, that both thou and thy seed may live: that thou mayest love the LORD thy God, obey His voice, cleave unto Him: for He is thy life, the length of thy days: that thou mayest dwell in the land which the LORD sware unto thy fathers. Guidance: 'Therefore choose life.' The most pleading verse in the Torah. God could compel; instead He asks. Heaven and earth witness the decision; your descendants depend on it. He is thy life — not a means to life, but life itself. To cleave to Him is the only choice that does not eventually choose death.
